The Dream
Packing Words
The Code
Getting There
Practice
Your Turn
Wrap-up
T
Day 1

Who Is Gleice?

Diagnostic Session & Personal Introduction

Let's Begin

Good Morning, Gleice!

In one word: what excites you most about starting your own business?
Tell me about your team at Sanofi. How many people?
If you could say ONE thing to a foreign business partner right now, what would it be?

About You

Name
What is your full name?
City
Where do you live?
Company
Where do you work?
Team
How many people do you manage?
A Goal
What do you want to achieve?
A Challenge
What is difficult for you?
Chapter 2

Packing Words

8 words for your professional world

Meet the Words

to be in charge of a team
Manage
to be in charge of a team or process
"I manage a team of thirty-four people."
to guide or direct a group
Lead
to guide or direct a group of people
"Gleice leads the transformation team."
to act or speak for a company
Represent
to act or speak for someone or a company
"I want to represent international brands."
a type of business or manufacturing
Industry
a type of business or manufacturing
"The pharmaceutical industry is competitive."
0 / 4 words revealed

More Words

a position or function in a company
Role
a position or function in a company
"My role is to improve processes."
a planned piece of work
Project
a planned piece of work with a specific goal
"I have a new project."
something you want to achieve
Goal
something you want to achieve
"My goal is to speak English."
something difficult that tests you
Challenge
something difficult that tests your abilities
"Speaking is a big challenge."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3

The Code

How present simple works

Discover the Pattern

I manage a large team.
She leads the department.
We represent international brands.
They work in the pharmaceutical industry.

What do the orange words have in common?

SubjectVerbExample
I / You / We / Theyverb (base)"I manage a team."
He / She / Itverb + s"She manages a team."

Common Mistake

"She manage a team."
"She manages a team."

With he, she, it — always add -s or -es to the verb.

Grammar Practice

Click to reveal the correct form:

Gleice ______ a team of 34 people.
???
manages
We ______ transformation projects.
???
lead
She ______ international brands.
???
represents
They ______ in the pharmaceutical industry.
???
work
My goal ______ to speak English well.
???
is
Chapter 4

Getting There

A real conversation at a networking event

At the Networking Event

M
Hi! I am Mark Thompson from PharmaTrade UK. Are you in the pharmaceutical industry too?
G
Yes! I am Gleice. I manage the Commercial Excellence team at Sanofi Medley in Brazil.
M
Interesting! What does your team do?
G
We lead transformation projects. My role is to improve commercial processes.
M
That sounds challenging. How big is your team?
G
I lead a team of thirty-four people. It is a big challenge, but I love it.
M
Impressive! Do you have any projects with international partners?
G
Yes! My goal is to represent international brands in the Brazilian market.

Did You Understand?

Where does Mark Thompson work?
He works at PharmaTrade UK.
What does Mark want to know about Gleice's team?
He wants to know what the team does and how big it is.
Is Mark interested in working with Gleice?
Yes, he asks about projects with international partners.

Listen Carefully

Gleice introduces herself at a conference

Listening 1
0:000:00
Where is Gleice from?
She is from Santo Andre, Brazil.
How many people does she manage?
She manages thirty-four people.
What is her new goal?
She wants to represent international brands in Brazil.

Business Card

SANOFI MEDLEY
Gleice Leonardo Rocha de Souza
Commercial Excellence & Transformation Manager
Santo Andre, SP — Brazil
Pharmaceutical Industry
Team: 34 people
Chapter 5

Practice

Use the words and grammar in action

Oral Drilling

Tell me your name and where you are from.
"My name is Gleice. I am from Santo Andre, Brazil."
Describe your job in one sentence.
"I manage the Commercial Excellence team at Sanofi Medley."
How many people are on your team?
"I lead a team of thirty-four people."
What industry do you work in?
"I work in the pharmaceutical industry."
What is your biggest professional goal?
"My goal is to represent international brands in Brazil."
What is your biggest challenge with English?
"My biggest challenge is speaking in real time."

Listen Again

Mark Thompson introduces himself

Listening 2
0:000:00
Where does Mark work?
He works at PharmaTrade UK.
How many people are on his team?
His team has twenty people.
What is his biggest challenge?
Finding the right partners in new markets.

Quick Fire

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Someone at a networking event asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself.
"I am Gleice. I manage the Commercial Excellence team at Sanofi Medley in Brazil."

Spot the Error

She manage a big team.
She manages a big team.
I works in the pharmaceutical industry.
I work in the pharmaceutical industry.
My goal are to represent brands.
My goal is to represent brands.
They leads transformation projects.
They lead transformation projects.
He have a team of twenty people.
He has a team of twenty people.
0 / 5 errors found
Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free — show what you learned

Role-Play: Guided

You are at an international business event. A partner from the UK asks about you. Use these keywords to answer:
GleiceSanto AndreSanofi Medley34 peoplepharmaceuticalrepresentinternational brands

Role-Play: Semi-Free

Same situation. Now with less support. Introduce yourself to a new business partner.
managegoalchallenge

Role-Play: Free

You are calling a potential international partner for the first time. Introduce yourself, explain your role, and tell them about your business project. No keywords. Just speak.
Record your introduction (optional):

Delayed Feedback

Errors from your practice today:

What you said
Correct version
What you said
Correct version
What you said
Correct version
Chapter 7

Wrap-up

What you learned today

Survival Card

1
Let me introduce myself. I am Gleice.
2
I manage the Commercial Excellence team.
3
My role is to lead transformation projects.
4
I work in the pharmaceutical industry in Brazil.
5
My goal is to represent international brands.

What I Learned

I can say my name and where I am from.
I can describe my job and my role.
I can talk about my team using present simple.
I can use subject pronouns correctly (I, she, we, they).
I can say my professional goal in English.
I can introduce myself to a business partner.
I can understand basic questions about my work.
I know 8 key words for professional introductions.

Day 1 Complete!

You introduced yourself in English.

"Nossa, tô engessada, hein? Oh my God. Mas, let's go." — You said it, Gleice. And you DID it.

Lesson Complete

Day 1 — Complete.

Next lesson: My World at Sanofi — Job & Team

Day 2

My World at Sanofi

Describing Your Job & Team

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Warm-up

Last class you learned 8 words. Can you remember 3?
Tell me about your role at Sanofi.
Who is on your team?
Chapter 2

Packing Words

8 new words for your professional world

New Words (1-4)

a division or section of a company
Department
a division or section of a company
"My department is Commercial Excellence."
a duty or task you must do
Responsibility
a duty or task you must do
"Her responsibility is to lead transformation projects."
to give information to someone above you
Report (to)
to give information to someone above you
"I report to the Commercial Director."
a person you work with
Colleague
a person you work with
"My colleagues and I improve commercial processes."
0 / 4 words revealed

New Words (5-8)

a series of steps to complete a task
Process
a series of steps to complete a task
"We are changing our sales processes."
a big change in how something works
Transformation
a big change in how something works
"The transformation of our department is going well."
a plan to achieve a long-term goal
Strategy
a plan to achieve a long-term goal
"Our strategy is to expand into new markets."
how well someone or something works
Performance
how well someone or something works
"The team's performance has been strong this year."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3

The Code

Possessives & 3rd Person Rules

Look at these sentences

My team works in Commercial Excellence.
Her responsibility is very important.
His strategy focuses on new markets.
Our process improves every quarter.
Their performance is excellent.

What do the orange words have in common?

Possessives

They show who something belongs to.

SubjectPossessiveExample
ImyMy department is Commercial Excellence.
youyourWhat are your responsibilities?
hehisHis role is to oversee the strategy.
sheherHer responsibility is to lead projects.
weourOur processes are improving.
theytheirTheir performance is strong.

Common Mistake

"She department is Commercial Excellence."

"Her department is Commercial Excellence."

Use possessives (my, your, his, her, our, their) before nouns, not subject pronouns.

3rd person -s: She reports to the Director. He manages the team.

Complete with the correct possessive

___ department is Commercial Excellence. (I)
???
My department is Commercial Excellence.
___ responsibility is to lead projects. (she)
???
Her responsibility is to lead projects.
___ role is to oversee the strategy. (he)
???
His role is to oversee the strategy.
___ processes are improving. (we)
???
Our processes are improving.
___ performance is strong this year. (they)
???
Their performance is strong this year.
Chapter 4

Getting There

Words in context & real conversation

Gleice meets David Park

International partner visiting Sanofi Medley

D
"I am visiting the Sanofi Medley office. Can you tell me about your department?"
G
"Of course! My department is Commercial Excellence. We have thirty-four colleagues."
D
"That is impressive! What are your main responsibilities?"
G
"My responsibility is to lead transformation projects. My colleagues and I improve our processes."
D
"Who do you report to?"
G
"I report to the Commercial Director. His role is to oversee our strategy."
D
"And how is your team's performance?"
G
"Our performance is strong. We are meeting all our goals this year."

About David Park

1. Why is David visiting?
He is visiting the Sanofi Medley office.
2. What did David ask about first?
He asked about Gleice's department.
3. What did David want to know about the team?
He asked about the team's performance.

Listening — Gleice describes her team

0:000:00
1. What is Gleice's department?
Commercial Excellence.
2. Who does she report to?
The Commercial Director.

Business Email

From: gleice.souza@sanofi.com
To: david.park@globalpharma.co.uk
Subject: Great meeting you today

Dear David,

Thank you for visiting our office today. It was great to meet you.

My department is Commercial Excellence. We have thirty-four colleagues on our team. My main responsibility is to lead transformation projects. I report to the Commercial Director. His role is to oversee our strategy.

Our team's performance has been strong this year. I believe our processes could benefit from your experience in international markets.

I look forward to working together.

Best regards,
Gleice Leonardo Rocha de Souza
Commercial Excellence Manager
Sanofi Medley

Chapter 5

Practice

Put your new words to work

Oral Practice

Describe your department.
"My department is Commercial Excellence."
What is your main responsibility?
"My responsibility is to lead transformation projects."
Who do you report to?
"I report to the Commercial Director."
How many colleagues do you have?
"Our team has thirty-four colleagues."
What is your team's strategy?
"Our strategy is to expand into new markets."
How is your team's performance?
"Our performance is strong. We are meeting all our goals this year."

Listening — David describes his team

0:000:00
1. Where does David work?
Global Pharma Partners in London.
2. Who does David report to?
The Managing Director.

Quick Fire

Question 1 of 6

Tell David about your department in one sentence.

"My department is Commercial Excellence."

0 / 6

Spot the Error

Find the possessive mistake. Click to reveal.

"She department has thirty-four colleagues."
"Her department has thirty-four colleagues."
"He strategy is to expand into new markets."
"His strategy is to expand into new markets."
"We performance is strong this year."
"Our performance is strong this year."
"She report to the Commercial Director."
"She reports to the Commercial Director." (3rd person -s)
"I report to he."
"I report to him." (object pronoun, not subject)

0 / 5 errors found

Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free — show what you learned

Role-play: Guided

David asks about your team

Use the keywords below to answer each question.

Commercial Excellence thirty-four transformation Commercial Director strong

Role-play: Semi-free

A new partner visits your office

Introduce yourself and describe your team. Use fewer hints.

department colleagues responsibility report to performance

Role-play: Free

An international visitor at your office

No keywords. Describe your department, team, responsibilities, and performance freely.

Delayed Feedback

Let us review together. Type what you said and the correct version.

Chapter 7

Wrap-up

Review & next steps

Survival Card — Day 2

1
My department is Commercial Excellence.
2
My responsibility is to lead transformation projects.
3
I report to the Commercial Director.
4
Our team has thirty-four colleagues.
5
Our performance is strong this year.

What I Learned

  • I can name my department in English.
  • I can describe my responsibilities.
  • I can say who I report to.
  • I can use possessives (my, her, his, our, their).
  • I can talk about my team's performance.
  • I can add -s to verbs with he/she/it.
  • I can write a short professional email.
  • I know 8 new words about my job and team.

Day 2 Badge Earned!

My World at Sanofi — Complete

Lesson Complete

Day 2 — Complete.

Next lesson: A Day in My Life — Daily Routine

Lesson 3

Day 3 — A Day in My Life

Daily Routines & Habits

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Warm-up

From Aula 2: What is your department? Who do you report to?
Describe your typical morning in one sentence.
How many meetings do you have today?
Chapter 2

Packing Words

8 new words for your daily professional life

New Words (1-4)

a plan that shows when things will happen
Schedule
a plan that shows when things will happen
"My schedule is very busy this week."
a time when people come together to discuss something
Meeting
a time when people come together to discuss something
"I have three meetings every day."
the last day or time to complete something
Deadline
the last day or time to complete something
"The deadline for the report is Friday."
to look at something again carefully
Review
to look at something again carefully
"I review our sales numbers every morning."
0 / 4 words revealed

New Words (5-8)

to travel between home and work
Commute
to travel between home and work
"I commute from Santo Andre to the office."
things you do regularly, in the same order
Routine
things you do regularly, in the same order
"My morning routine starts at six."
to decide which tasks are most important
Prioritize
to decide which tasks are most important
"I always prioritize my team's needs."
to organize people or activities to work together
Coordinate
to organize people or activities to work together
"I coordinate projects across departments."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3

The Code

Present Simple & Frequency Adverbs

Look at these sentences

I always review the numbers.
She usually has three meetings.
We sometimes work late.
He never misses a deadline.

What do the orange words have in common?

Frequency Adverbs

Subject + ADVERB + verb

AdverbFrequencyExample
always100%I always review my schedule.
usually80%She usually starts at seven.
often60%We often have long meetings.
sometimes40%He sometimes works late.
never0%I never miss a deadline.

Time expressions go at the end: every day, in the morning, at night.

Common Mistake

"I go always to meetings."

"I always go to meetings."

Rule: Subject + ADVERB + verb. The adverb goes BEFORE the main verb.

Complete with the correct adverb

I ___ review my schedule before meetings. (100%)
???
I always review my schedule before meetings.
She ___ starts at seven in the morning. (80%)
???
She usually starts at seven in the morning.
We ___ work late on Fridays. (40%)
???
We sometimes work late on Fridays.
He ___ misses a deadline. (0%)
???
He never misses a deadline.
I ___ coordinate projects with other teams. (60%)
???
I often coordinate projects with other teams.
Chapter 4

Getting There

Words in context & real conversation

Gleice talks with Sarah

Colleague from the London office

S
"Good morning, Gleice! What does your typical day look like?"
G
"I usually start at seven. I review my schedule and prioritize my tasks."
S
"That is early! How do you commute to work?"
G
"I commute from Santo Andre. It usually takes about forty minutes."
S
"Do you have many meetings?"
G
"Yes! I always have at least three meetings a day. I coordinate projects with different departments."
S
"How do you manage your deadlines?"
G
"I never miss a deadline. I always check my calendar and prioritize what is urgent."

About Sarah

1. What does Sarah want to know about Gleice?
She wants to know about Gleice's typical day.
2. Does Sarah think Gleice starts early?
Yes, she says "That is early!"
3. What does Sarah ask about deadlines?
She asks "How do you manage your deadlines?"

Listening — Gleice describes her day

0:000:00
1. What does Gleice always do first in the morning?
She reviews her schedule.
2. How long is her commute?
About forty minutes.

Gleice's Daily Schedule

Monday — Gleice Leonardo R. de Souza
6:00Wake up — morning routine
6:40Commute from Santo Andre
7:00Review schedule + prioritize tasks
8:00Meeting 1 — Team alignment
9:30Meeting 2 — Coordinate with Sales
11:00Review reports + check deadlines
12:00Lunch
13:00Meeting 3 — Strategy review
17:00Commute home
Chapter 5

Practice

Put your words to work

Quick Drill

Answer each situation out loud

1. What time do you usually start your day?
2. How do you commute to work?
3. What do you always do first in the morning?
4. How many meetings do you have every day?
5. Do you ever miss a deadline?
6. What do you prioritize every morning?

Listening — Sarah's routine in London

0:000:00
1. What time does Sarah usually start?
Eight thirty.
2. How does Sarah commute?
By train, about thirty minutes.

Quick Fire

Question 1 of 6

Someone asks: "What time do you start your day?" Answer using a frequency adverb.

"I usually start my day at seven in the morning."

Score: 0 / 6

Spot the Error

Find the mistake and click to see the correction. 0 / 5 errors found

"I go always to meetings."
"I always go to meetings." (adverb before the verb)
"She has usually three meetings."
"She usually has three meetings." (adverb before the verb)
"We work sometimes late."
"We sometimes work late." (adverb before the verb)
"He misses never a deadline."
"He never misses a deadline." (adverb before the verb)
"I review always my schedule."
"I always review my schedule." (adverb before the verb)
Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free — show what you learned

Role-play: Guided

Situation

Sarah asks you about your daily routine at Sanofi. Answer each question using the keywords below.

seven schedule three meetings Santo Andre deadline

Teacher plays Sarah. Student answers using keywords.

Role-play: Semi-free

Situation

You are at a conference. A new colleague asks: "What does your typical day look like?" Describe your full routine using at least 3 frequency adverbs.

always usually never every day

Role-play: Free

Free Speaking

Describe your entire daily routine from morning to night. Include your commute, schedule, meetings, and deadlines. Speak for 60 seconds.

Delayed Error Correction

Type the errors from the role-play. Then write the correction.

Chapter 7

Wrap-up

Key phrases & reflection

Survival Card — Lesson 3

1
I usually start my day at seven in the morning.
2
I always prioritize my team's needs.
3
I have three meetings every day.
4
I never miss a deadline.
5
I commute from Santo Andre to the office.

What I Learned

  • I can describe my daily routine in English.
  • I can use frequency adverbs (always, usually, sometimes, never).
  • I can talk about my commute and schedule.
  • I can say when and how often I do things.
  • I can describe my meetings and deadlines.
  • I can use time expressions (every day, in the morning).
  • I can coordinate and prioritize in English.
  • I know 8 key words for daily professional life.

Day 3 Badge Earned!

A Day in My Life — Complete

schedule meeting deadline routine commute prioritize coordinate review
Lesson Complete

Day 3 — Complete.

Next lesson: Questions That Connect

LESSON 1

Complementary Materials — Lesson 1

Watch, listen, and practice outside of class. Check each one when you finish.

Lesson 1 — Who Is Gleice?

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1

Corporate vocabulary, negotiations, professional introductions. Watch how Harvey introduces himself.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Pause when you hear a new word.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Easy English Podcast — How to Introduce Yourself & Make a Great First Impression in English

A clear listening episode on how to introduce yourself professionally: key phrases, how to keep the conversation going, and common mistakes to avoid. Clear, learner-friendly English.

Listen once without pausing, then again while repeating the key phrases.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Speak English With Vanessa — Self-Introduction

How to introduce yourself in English. Clear American accent, natural phrases, short format.

Tip: After watching, record yourself using the same structure.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — My World at Sanofi

Series
Shark Tank — Season 1, Episode 1

Entrepreneurs describe their teams, responsibilities, and business strategies. Great for team vocabulary.

Tip: Listen to how they describe their roles and team structure.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
5-minute English Practice — [B1] Talking About Your Job

A short conversation between Lisa and Emily about their jobs, teams, and responsibilities, with useful workplace vocabulary and natural phrases to ask and answer 'What do you do?'. Clear, slow English.

Pause after each part and describe your own job using the same phrases.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy — How to Describe Your Job

Professional phrases for describing your role, responsibilities, and team in a business setting.

Tip: Write down 3 phrases from the video and use them to describe your job at Sanofi.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — A Day in My Life

Movie
The Intern (2015)

A professional woman managing her daily routine at a busy company. Clear American English, office vocabulary, daily life context.

Tip: Watch the first 30 minutes. Notice how characters describe their daily routines and schedules.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
5-minute English Practice — [A2] My Daily Routine in English

Anna describes her daily routine in clear, natural A2 English — a great model for talking about your own workday, schedule, and tasks. Simple sentences you can copy.

Listen once, then try to describe your own routine using the same phrases.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
All Ears English — How to Describe Your Daily Routine

Natural phrases for talking about your daily habits and routines. Frequency adverbs in real conversation.

Tip: After watching, record yourself describing your routine using at least 3 frequency adverbs.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Questions That Connect

Série
The Office (US) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

No primeiro episódio, os personagens se apresentam e fazem perguntas de trabalho: "What do you do here?", "Where do you work?". Otimo para ouvir Wh-questions no escritório.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote toda pergunta que começar com What, Where, How, Why.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

YouTube
The Office (US) — Official Trailer

The official trailer for The Office (US), full of the fast, natural workplace English and dry humor that make it great listening practice for everyday register and idioms.

Assista primeiro sem legenda para treinar o ouvido, depois de novo com legenda em ingles para pegar as expressoes.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 552: How to Continue Small Talk at the Start of a Work Call

Duas professoras do All Ears English mostram como puxar conversa e manter o small talk no comeco de uma ligacao de trabalho, com perguntas naturais. Otimo para ouvir Wh-questions em contexto profissional.

Escolha duas perguntas do episodio e use no proximo bate-papo no trabalho.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Lesson 5 — Block 1 Review -- Confidence Check

Série
Superstore -- Season 1, Episode 1

Comédia leve em um ambiente de trabalho americano: apresentações, colegas, rotinas e perguntas do dia a dia — as mesmas estruturas do seu Bloco 1, em conversas reais e curtas.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote toda frase com "What do you...?" ou "She works...".

Disponível no streaming: Prime Video

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "How to introduce yourself" (English at Work)

Vídeos curtos da BBC sobre apresentações no trabalho — a missão da sua simulação. Inglês claro e pausado, perfeito para revisar antes de gravar sua reflexão em áudio.

Dica: pause após cada frase-modelo e repita em voz alta, trocando pelos SEUS dados (nome, Sanofi, seu time).

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 552: How to Continue Small Talk at the Start of a Work Call

Episodio do All Ears English sobre conversas informais no trabalho: como comecar e continuar o small talk numa ligacao ou reuniao. Voce vai ouvir em uso real as perguntas da Aula 4.

Ouca uma vez e repita as perguntas de small talk em voz alta.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Lesson 6 — My Career Story -- Simple Past (Regular Verbs)

Series
The Bold Type -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Three young women talk about how their careers started and grew. Listen for simple past verbs: started, studied, joined, moved, applied. Notice how each woman tells the story of where she began.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences in the simple past about a character's career. Then record one sentence about your own career using -ed.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
How I Built This — Starbucks: Howard Schultz

Howard Schultz tells his story step by step: discovering a small Seattle coffee shop in 1981, the jobs and decisions along the way, and buying and transforming Starbucks into a global chain.

Follow the sequence of moves in the past simple — where he started and each step he took.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Learn the Simple Past | Regular Verbs

A short, clear lesson on the simple past of regular verbs: when to add -ed, +d, and how to change y to ied (study to studied, apply to applied). Directly useful for telling your own career story.

After watching, write your career timeline with five sentences in the simple past, then record a 60-second audio telling your story.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Key Moments -- Simple Past (Irregular Verbs)

Series
The Founder -- a film about turning points

A real story of the key moments that built a company. Listen for irregular past verbs: went, got, made, left, became, took, won, found. Notice how one big decision changed everything.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences in the simple past with irregular verbs about a turning point. Then record one sentence about a key moment in your own career.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
How I Built This — LinkedIn: Reid Hoffman

Reid Hoffman describes the turning points of his career: early failed social-network attempts, the decision to co-found LinkedIn, and the moves that led to its $26 billion sale to Microsoft.

Notice the key decisions and moments that changed the direction of his story.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Past simple irregular verbs - English grammar lessons

A short, clear lesson on the simple past of irregular verbs: go to went, get to got, make to made, take to took. It also shows how to make questions with did plus the base verb (Did you go?).

After watching, write five key moments of your career with irregular past verbs, then record a 60-second audio telling your three biggest turning points.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — The Pharma World -- Industry Vocabulary (Present Simple)

Series / Documentary
Explained -- an episode about the medicine and pharma industry

A clear look at how a company develops a product, supplies pharmacies, and launches a new treatment for patients. Listen for present simple verbs with -s: develops, supplies, sells, launches, helps.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences in the present simple about what a pharma company does. Use the new words: patient, treatment, product, market, pharmacy, brand, supply, launch.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
The Readout LOUD (STAT) — Viking's Obesity Flop, 'Pharma to Table' Drug Sales

In this STAT weekly biotech episode, the hosts break down Viking Therapeutics' obesity-drug setback and Big Pharma's direct-to-consumer 'pharma to table' sales push and whether it lowers prices.

It's fast, real-news English — replay tricky sections and jot down key pharma terms.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Present Simple Tense (Third Person "s")

A short, clear lesson on the present simple with -s: he develops, she sells, it launches. It also shows how to make questions and negatives with does plus the base verb (Does the company sell?).

After watching, write five sentences about your world at Sanofi with present simple verbs, then record a 60-second audio describing what your company develops, supplies, and launches.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Meetings at Sanofi -- Basic Meeting Language (Can / Could)

Series / Business
The Office -- a meeting scene

A short meeting scene shows how a team starts a meeting, follows an agenda, and makes a decision together. Listen for meeting words: agenda, topic, update, decision, break, and for can and could in polite requests.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 polite requests with can or could that you hear (for example, "Can everyone see the screen?"). Then write your own agenda with 3 topics.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Why Meetings Go Wrong (And How to Fix Them)

Researcher Steven Rogelberg explains how a good manager prepares an agenda, runs the meeting on time, listens to the team, and reaches a decision. Packed with real meeting phrases used at work.

Write down five meeting phrases (agenda, on time, decision, follow up) as you hear them.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Learn "Can" & "Could" Easily | English Grammar Lesson (Abilities & Polite Requests) | A2 Level

A short, clear lesson on can and could for polite requests and offers: Can you send the agenda? Could you repeat that, please? It shows the difference between can (more direct) and could (more polite).

After watching, write five polite requests with can and could that you can use in your next meeting at Sanofi. Then record a 60-second audio where you open a meeting and present a short agenda.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Block 2 Review -- Progress Check (aulas 6 to 9)

Series / Business
The Office -- a career and meeting recap

Choose one episode and watch a team review their results: how they started a project, what they launched, and how they run their meetings. You will hear the simple past, the present simple, and polite requests with can and could -- all the block 2 grammar together.

Watch with English subtitles. Write 3 past sentences (what happened) and 2 present sentences (what the team does now). Then say them out loud.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Navigate Change at Any Career Stage

Bonnie Hammer tells the story of her career, rising from the lowest-level production assistant to vice chair of NBCUniversal: how she got noticed, earned promotions, and became a leader. A real 'my career journey' narrative.

Notice past-tense verbs she uses to tell her story (started, got promoted, became).

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
The Simple Present vs. the Simple Past

A short, clear lesson that compares the simple past (I started, I became) with the present simple (the company supplies). It also reviews questions with did and polite requests with can and could -- the whole block 2 toolbox in one video.

After watching, record a 90-second audio: tell your career story in the past, describe your team today in the present, and finish with a polite request using could.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — First Contact -- Phone & Email (Present Continuous)

Series / Business
The Office -- a phone call scene

A short scene shows people answering the phone, taking a message, and saying who is calling. Listen for first contact phrases and for the present continuous: "I am calling about...", "she is answering the phone".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences in the present continuous that you hear (for example, "He is calling the client"). Then say out loud what you are doing right now.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 347: Not Allowed to Answer? What to Say in English

A short telephone-English episode on what to say on a work call when you can't give an answer right away — how to respond politely and keep the call moving. Listen for real phone phrases.

Practice saying two of the phone responses out loud before your next call.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
Present Continuous | An Easy English Grammar Lesson

A short, clear lesson on the present continuous: am / is / are + verb-ing for actions happening now. It shows the difference between "I work here" (every day) and "I am working now" (right now).

After watching, write five present continuous sentences about your first contact at work (for example, "I am replying to an email"). Then record a 60-second audio where you make a first contact call and leave a short message.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — My Business Idea -- Elevator Pitch (Going to)

Series / Business
Shark Tank -- a short pitch scene

A short scene shows an entrepreneur giving an elevator pitch: the idea, the customer, the problem, and the plan. Listen for going to: "I am going to launch...", "it is going to grow".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences with going to that you hear (for example, "We are going to open a new store"). Then describe your own business idea out loud.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart — 185. Say Less, Mean More: Craft Compelling Communication

Guy Kawasaki shows how to present an idea in as few words as possible: open strong, state the problem and the solution clearly, and make the ask.

After listening, try pitching one of your own ideas in three short sentences.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Using "Going To" for Future Plans | Easy English Grammar Lesson

A short, clear lesson on going to: am / is / are + going to + base verb for plans. It shows the difference between "I work here" (every day) and "I am going to start a business" (a plan for the future).

After watching, write five going to sentences about your plans at work (for example, "I am going to launch a new service"). Then record a 90-second audio: give your elevator pitch.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Describing Products -- What We Offer (Comparatives)

Series / Business
Shark Tank -- a short product comparison scene

A short scene shows an entrepreneur describing a product and comparing it with another one. Listen for comparatives: "faster than", "more reliable than", "cheaper than", "better than".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 comparative sentences that you hear (for example, "It is faster than the others"). Then describe a product from your work out loud.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Learning English For Work — Office English: Pitching

A short BBC episode where Pippa and Phil show how to pitch something at work: presenting features and benefits and making your idea or product sound convincing.

Copy the phrases used to highlight a benefit and say them out loud.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
Learn English Comparatives in 7 Minutes: Adjectives with -ER and MORE

A short, clear lesson on comparatives: short adjective + er + than (faster than, cheaper than), more + adjective + than for long words (more reliable than), and irregular forms (good becomes better).

After watching, write five comparative sentences about products at work (for example, "Our new model is more affordable than the old one"). Then record a one-minute audio: describe and compare your product.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — The Best Choice -- Why We Are the Best (Superlatives)

Series / Business
Shark Tank -- a pitch for the best product

A short scene shows an entrepreneur recommending a product as the number one choice. Listen for superlatives: "the fastest", "the most reliable", "the best", "the most popular".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 superlative sentences that you hear (for example, "It is the best on the market"). Then recommend a product from your work out loud.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 481: Make a Push to Learn These Business Phrases

A short episode with practical business phrases you can use to put an idea forward and make a suggestion or recommendation at work in natural English.

Choose two phrases and use them to recommend something in your next meeting.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
Superlative Adjectives – English Grammar Lessons

A short, clear lesson on superlatives: the + short adjective + est (the fastest, the cheapest), the most + adjective for long words (the most reliable), and irregular forms (good becomes the best).

After watching, write five superlative sentences about products at work (for example, "Our product is the most reliable on the market"). Then record a one-minute audio: recommend your best product.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Sharing Good News -- We Have Done It (Present Perfect)

Series / Business
The Office (US) -- a company announcement scene

A short scene shows a manager announcing results to the team. Listen for the present perfect: "we have reached", "we have done", "the team has worked", and words like result, goal, and success.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 present perfect sentences that you hear (for example, "We have reached our target"). Then announce one real result from your team out loud.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
Down to Business English — Nokiasoft: Microsoft acquires Nokia | 65

The hosts break down Microsoft's acquisition of Nokia's phone business — a real corporate announcement, walking through the deal and what it meant for both companies.

Notice how the hosts report a company's big news; reuse phrases like "the company announced" and "the deal is worth."

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
The Present Perfect Tense | English Grammar Lesson

A short, clear lesson on the present perfect: have / has + past participle (we have launched, the team has worked), regular verbs with -ed, and irregular forms (do becomes done, win becomes won).

After watching, write five present perfect sentences about results at work (for example, "We have achieved our main goal"). Then record a one-minute audio: announce your team's good news.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Looking Back, Looking Ahead -- Checkpoint (aulas 11 to 15)

Series / Business
The Office -- a full business review scene

Choose one episode and watch a team review their business: what they are doing now, their plans, how they compare products, and the results they have reached. You will hear the present continuous, going to, comparatives, superlatives, and the present perfect -- the whole business block together.

Watch with English subtitles. Write 1 sentence for each tool: now, a plan, a comparison, the best, and a result. Then say them out loud.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 481: Make a Push to Learn These Business Phrases

Lindsay and Aubrey walk through practical business phrases you can use in meetings and reviews to sound clear and professional — a useful model for running your own quarterly review at Sanofi.

Note down two phrases from the episode and use them out loud in your next update.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
Verb Tenses: Present Simple, Present Continuous, Past Simple, Present Perfect and more

A short, clear review that compares the present continuous (I am calling) with going to (I am going to launch) and the present perfect (I have reached). It also reviews comparatives and superlatives -- the whole business block toolbox in one place.

After watching, record a 90-second business review: say what your team is doing now, give a future plan, compare and recommend your product, and finish with a result in the present perfect.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Talking About Experiences -- present perfect & past simple

Series / Interview
A job interview scene -- talking about experiences

Choose a scene where someone talks about their career experiences. You will hear the present perfect for experiences (I have worked, I have travelled) and the past simple for finished moments (last year, I went). Notice when they use each tense.

Watch with English subtitles. Write 3 present perfect sentences (experiences) and 2 past simple sentences (finished moments). Then say them out loud about yourself.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
All Ears English — AEE 2184: 3 Tips for Telling Better Stories in English

The hosts share three techniques for telling stories about what you have done — trips, projects and experiences — so your listener stays engaged. A perfect model for talking about clients you have visited and deals you have closed.

Pick one work experience and retell it out loud using the episode's three story tips.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
Present Perfect or Past Simple? - English Grammar lesson

A short, clear lesson that compares the present perfect (I have visited) for experiences with the past simple (I visited last year) for finished moments. It also shows ever, never, already, and yet in action.

After watching, record a 90-second career story: three experiences with the present perfect and one finished moment with the past simple.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Talking About Your Career Story -- present perfect with for & since

Series / Interview
A job interview scene -- telling your career story

Choose a scene where someone tells how long they have done their job. You will hear the present perfect with for (I have worked here for ten years) and since (I have been a manager since 2018). Notice when they use for and when they use since.

Watch with English subtitles. Write 3 sentences with for and 2 sentences with since. Then say them out loud about your own career.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 557: Did a New Job Get Off to a Rocky Start? How to Talk About It

Lindsay and Aubrey model the language for talking about a job and career path — how things started, what you do now, and how to describe your background naturally. A perfect model for telling your own story at Sanofi.

Listen once for the storyline, then a second time writing down the past-tense verbs they use.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
FOR and SINCE with the present perfect in English | Grammar lesson

A short, clear lesson on how to say how long with the present perfect: for + a period of time (for five years) and since + a start point (since 2015). It shows many examples for your career story.

After watching, record a 90-second career story: how long you have worked here (for), since when you have been in your position (since), and your background and field.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Job Interviews -- answering "Tell me about yourself"

Series / Film
A job interview scene -- answering "Tell me about yourself"

Choose a scene with a job interview. Listen for how the candidate answers "Tell me about yourself": they give their background and use the present perfect (I have worked, I have managed). Notice how confident and honest they sound.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down the candidate's answer to "Tell me about yourself" and one strength and one weakness they mention.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Real English Conversations — English Job Interviews with All Ears English's Lindsay McMahon (Part 2)

Lindsay McMahon from All Ears English shares what employers really look for: how to talk about your strengths, handle tricky questions, and be the dream candidate. A perfect model for your own interview practice.

Pause after each tip and say your own answer out loud before continuing.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
How to Answer "Tell Me About Yourself" in a Job Interview (SEAT Method)

A short, clear lesson on the most common interview question. It shows how to give your background, use the present perfect for your experience, and end with confidence. Great preparation for a real interview.

After watching, record a 90-second interview answer: your background, how long you have worked in your field, one strength, one weakness, and why you are the right candidate.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Checkpoint 4 -- The Full Story (aulas 17 to 19)

Series / Business
Suits -- a job interview scene

Choose one interview scene and watch a candidate talk about their experience and career. You will hear the present perfect (I have worked...), the past simple (I started...), and for and since for duration -- the whole Experiences block together.

Watch with English subtitles. Write 1 sentence for each tool: a life experience, a finished moment, a duration with for, and a start point with since. Then say them out loud.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
All Ears English — AEE 105: Three Phrases to Tell Your Story in English Like a Native Speaker

The hosts give you three phrases to introduce and tell your own story naturally — exactly what you need to answer "Tell me about yourself" in an interview. A perfect model for your own answer.

Build a 30-second 'tell me about yourself' answer using the three phrases from the episode.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
Past Simple or Present Perfect? – English Grammar lesson

A short, clear review that compares the present perfect (I have visited) with the past simple (I started in 2012), and shows how to use for (a duration) and since (a start point) -- the whole Experiences block toolbox in one place.

After watching, record a 90-second job interview answer: a life experience, a finished moment, your time here with for and since, and your biggest strength.

Assistir no YouTube ↗
